_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to select appropriate equipment and use it to generate and record data with attention to precision.

_source.w: We are learning to select appropriate equipment and use it to generate and record data with attention to precision.
y: Choosing the right instrument for a measurement — and using it correctly — directly affects the reliability of our data and conclusions.
Understanding precision and significant figures helps us communicate how confident we are in our measurements, which is essential in any scientific field.
These practical skills are used daily by professionals in medicine, engineering, environmental monitoring and trades.
eg: Choosing a graduated cylinder rather than a beaker to measure liquid volumes because it provides greater precision.
Using a digital balance accurate to 0.01 g rather than a kitchen scale when measuring small quantities of a chemical.
Recording temperature readings from a probe to one decimal place and noting the uncertainty of the instrument.
Selecting a light meter to measure intensity in lux rather than estimating brightness by eye.
Using a microscope with the correct magnification to observe cell structures and recording observations with labelled diagrams.
Taking repeated measurements of reaction time and recording all values in a properly formatted table with units.
